content.full_text:
0sc 13 3ls Pii '38
December ll, 1990
Ms. Susan Bobrow
Legal Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, Califomia 95814
Re: 
Conflicts of lnterest
Dear Susan:
As we discussed, I am requesting a clarificatior on some areas of the conflict of i-oterest rules I do not fully
understand.
I am a Planning Commissioner for The City of Bakersfield (City) and an employee of DeWalt Corporation
(DeWalt) which provides civil engineering/surveying services.
There are two major housing developments in the City which are adjacent to each other (see map). DeWalt
has a contract for services with Riverlakes (the easterly project). The Polo Grounds (the westedy project) is going
through various review/approval proceedings before the City Plarning Commission. The question I have is as
follows:
Q1 - 
As an employee of a company doing work for a project, can I legally vote on an adjaceot project
(within 300 feet) of the project my company is working on?
I would also like to know if my conflict status would change if I owned stocks in DeWalt or was
a principal or had other ownership and management interest in the corporation. Also, would my
status change if I were not an employee but only did contract work for DeWalt?
Q2 - 
If I am personally not working on the adjacent project would I have a legal conflict? Would my
status change considering stock and/or ownership interest noted above?
Q3 - 
If DeWalt is providing services for a project under review but is not the owner or the applicant
on the project woulci I have a legal conilict of interest?
Some of these questions are beyond the scope of our discussion, but I would like a better understanding
of conflicts of interest for future reference.
Thank you for your help. If you have any questions don't hesitate to call.
Sincerely,
&-
Jim Marino
805) 871-3300 (Work)

sections.question:
Q1 - As an employee of a company doing work for a project, can I legally vote on an adjacent project (within 300 feet) of the project my company is working on? I would also like to know if my conflict status would change if I owned stocks in DeWalt or was a principal or had other ownership and management interest in the corporation. Also, would my status change if I were not an employee but only did contract work for DeWalt?

Q2 - If I am personally not working on the adjacent project would I have a legal conflict? Would my status change considering stock and/or ownership interest noted above?

Q3 - If DeWalt is providing services for a project under review but is not the owner or the applicant on the project would I have a legal conflict of interest?
sections.question_synthetic: A Planning Commissioner for the City of Bakersfield who works as an employee of DeWalt Corporation (which has a contract for civil engineering/surveying services on one housing development) seeks clarification on whether he can vote on an adjacent housing development project and how his conflict status would change based on different employment relationships and ownership interests in DeWalt.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter from the public to FPPC staff member Susan Bobrow, not an FPPC response or advice letter. No CONCLUSION/SHORT ANSWER section exists. Document contains three specific questions (Q1, Q2, Q3) about conflicts of interest. Letter is dated December 11, 1990 and includes contact information and a map reference.

I would also like to know if my conflict status would change if I owned stocks in DeWalt or was a principal or had other ownership and management interest in the corporation. Also, would my status change if I were not an employee but only did contract work for DeWalt?

Q2 - If I am personally not working on the adjacent project would I have a legal conflict? Would my status change considering stock and/or ownership interest noted above?

Q3 - If DeWalt is providing services for a project under review but is not the owner or the applicant on the project would I have a legal conflict of interest?

embedding.summary: Jim Marino, a Planning Commissioner for the City of Bakersfield and employee of DeWalt Corporation, requests clarification from the FPPC on conflict of interest rules regarding his ability to vote on a housing development project adjacent to one that his employer is working on.
